comment_content:
COMP2633 offers diverse, week-by-week cybersecurity topics that progress from foundational CTF skills to advanced exploitation techniques. The first two weeks provide basic training with hands-on exercises to familiarize students with the competitive programming environment and fundamental CTF concepts.

The course follows a dual-track system where Track A covers binary exploitation topics (Reverse Engineering, PWN, symbolic execution) while Track B focuses on application security (Web Security, Cryptography, SQL Injections, Forensics).

Topics include advanced exploitation techniques like ROP (Return-Oriented Programming), format string attacks, RSA cryptanalysis, shellcode development, and server-side attacks. This structure exposes students to comprehensive cybersecurity domains used in real CTF competitions.

Week 0: Course Introduction
Week 1: Basic Training - Linux 101
Week 2: Basic Training - Network, Python, and PWN
Week 3: Reverse 101 (Track A), Web Security 101 (Track B)
Week 4: PWN 101 (Track A), Crypto 101 (Track B)
Week 5: Reverse 102 (Track A), SQL Injections 101 (Track B)
Week 6: PWN 102: Shellcode and lazy binding (Track A), Cryptanalysis (Track B)
Week 7: PWN 103 Return Oriented Programming (Track A), Client-Side Attacks (Track B)
Week 8: tryHarder on your midterms
Week 9: PWN 105 Format String Attacks (idk where did PWN 104 go) (Track A), Fantastic RSA and How to Attack Them (Track B)
Week 10: Stack migration (Track A), Server side attacks (Track B)
Week 11: Grab some coffee for continuing your CTF life
Week 12: Symbolic Execution (Track A), Forensics and Steganography (Track B)
comment_teaching:
Dr. Alex Lam does not directly teach the course; instead, COMP4633 students (HKUST Firebird Core members) serve as "Helpers" who lead instruction. While these peer instructors cover fundamental concepts and provide helpful explanations, the course heavily emphasizes self-directed learning.

The helpers demonstrate genuine effort to explain basics and problem-solving approaches, but you must independently bridge the gap between foundational knowledge and the advanced techniques required for homework completion—Google is your best friend. These peer instructors are valuable resources who showcase diverse problem-solving methodologies and can significantly accelerate learning for motivated students.

Since this is an online course, active participation in the online class chat room and the Firebird Discord channel is essential for connecting with experienced members and potential future teammates. Don't be shy—all the "bigbigs" (high-achievers and experienced CTF players) are approachable and friendly. The Firebird community functions like a big family where members share daily experiences and willingly help others when asked. This collaborative environment provides invaluable learning opportunities beyond formal course materials.
comment_workload:
Workload perception varies significantly based on individual goals. Students aiming merely to pass with minimal CTF engagement may find the workload manageable, while those competing for selection into COMP3633 (Firebird school CTF team) face substantially higher demands, so i gave it an average grade: C.

For students with serious competitive ambitions—like me determined to join the Firebird school team—the workload extends far beyond course requirements. These motivated students invest substantial effort into external CTF competitions, applying skills learned from the course and COMP4633 peer instructors to earn rankings and recognition.

The intensive self-study and competition participation, while demanding, yields tangible rewards through competition placements and demonstrates the practical value of knowledge gained from both course materials and advanced student mentors.​

The competitive environment attracts high-achieving students, including Dean's List recipients, creating opportunities for peer learning and collaboration with skilled teammates. Weekly exercises with 2-week deadlines require continuous engagement, and the self-study component demands significant time investment for students unfamiliar with security concepts. The course serves as preparation for competitive cybersecurity events, making the workload proportional to one's competitive ambitions rather than course requirements alone.
comment_grading:
The course uses a Pass/Fail grading system requiring only 60% out of a total 150% to pass. The breakdown is generous:
​
- 50% for class attendance

- 50% for Track A exercises and homework

- 50% for Track B exercises and homework

Although I was on study leave and couldn't officially register, I stayed active and ranked No. 2 on the training platform by solving most challenges. From my experience, you can easily pass just by maintaining full attendance and grabbing the accessible "free flag" exercises without needing to master every hard challenge.

I honestly think being a 0-credit course is a huge advantage. There is absolutely no pressure to fight for a letter grade or view other COMP2633 students as competitors. Instead, the environment encourages everyone to share ideas and help each other grow, since helping a classmate doesn't hurt your own chances of passing.

comment_content:
In this course, there are 2 tracks: track A and track B. The content of the tracks are different. You have to choose at least 1 track.

In Track A, you will learn about reverse and PWN. I choose track B so I doesn't have much to say.

In Track B, you will learn about Web, Crypto and some forensics. If you wish to take track B, taking COMP 2711H will be extremely helpful for Crypto. COMP 2711 also helps as well.

I have to say in the majority of the course, what you learn in class won't directly help you finish you homework, especially the first 2 weeks. You have to conduct your own research online, which is common in CTF competitions. Instead, the teching gives a framework for organizing knowledge, as well as some directions for you to conduct your own researches.
comment_teaching:
The teaching have little impact on your learning. At the end of the day, self-learning is required and encouraged.

The courses are teached by COMP 4633 students, all of them are highly skilled CTF players. The lecture takes around 2 hours for a track. I don't suggest you taking both tracks, but if you do, there are recording of the lessons in the course web.

There is also a welcoming CTF event (which is really fun) and a discord server for this course. I highly suggest you to join both, especially the discord server where you can worship bigbigs and ask questions about CTF directly. You may also find writeups of past assignments and enchance your knowledge as well.
comment_workload:
I love CTF, so I think the workload is moderate. But if you don't like CTF, this will be pure hell.

You have 2-3 questions a week for 1 track. You will never know how long does it takes to finish one, and the problem-solving process can be extremely discouraging (for example week 7 track b hw2).

However, the questions are quite interesting and challenging. I am sure that solving these questions are benefitial.
comment_grading:
Pass or fail only, so chill if you accidentally enroll this course and fail to drop. To get a pass, you will need to get at least 60% of the total scores. You can achieve this by handing in all attendance flag and homework flag.

For those who are hoping to achieve more (namely COMP 3633), pass is not enough. You have to perform well in Firebird internal CTF.

comment_content:
Course content can be found on https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2633/

This course covers a lot of topics, from binary exploits to cryptography and web web security. It will certainly be tough if you have not much previous knowledge (As it's like a bunch of courses scrambled into one).

You can chose from two tracks:
Track A - binary exploits, reverse engineering
Track B - Web, cryptography, forensics
After add-drop period, each track will include a 2 to 3-hour online lecture every week, although occasionally there will be breaks (e.g. during mid-terms, competitions)
comment_teaching:
Teaching is done by COMP4633 students, who are very talented and actively participate large-scale CTF competitions.

There will be 1 online lecture per week (except for break periods). Usually it's 4-6 hours long at night. Technically, you do not have to attend the lectures and you can watch the lecture videos online. Either way, I suggest you actually follow the lectures, and get hands-on experience as they teach. Only by that will you be able to learn valuable CTF skills.

The helpers are very helpful and they will assist you on Discord when you don't mind reaching them for help in private chats.
comment_workload:
Tldr: The more you want to learn, the higher the workload

If you want to complete most of the challenges (For people who aim for taking COMP3633, this is a must), you will need to spend a lot of time every week. Some homework challenges will surely take hours to solve.

However, if all you need is a pass, the workload for this course is low.  

Considering this is a 0-credit course, I will rate this an E.
comment_grading:
Grading is solely based on online CTF exercises completion.

For every week, usually, there will be:
* 2 Attendance challenges - 1 in track A, 1 in track B, you only need to solve 1. They will guide you step-by-step on lectures and they often provide you the flag (the solution) during lectures.
* 1-2 Exercises per track - they will lead you to complete it during lectures and they sometimes provide you the flag
* 1-2 Homework per track

Completing all attendance will give you 50 marks, and (exercises + homework) worth 50 marks per track.
The passing score is 60, so you do not need to complete a lot of tasks to actually get a pass.

The homework, especially those after mid-term periods, are significantly hard to solve. In this term, there is one homework challenges left unsolved (No one was able to solve it) and many challenges with less than 10 solves.

comment_content:
Since every other review is warning people who haven't played CTF, I write this review for the future students who have already played CTF, as I have (poorly as it may be) in secondary school.
If my review isn't sufficient, you can dm me @wylited on discord.

Alongside COMP2711H, this is the best course I've taken in my first semester at HKUST.

This course is pretty invaluable in improving my skills in CTF and competitive cybersecurity, as I have never formally studied these topics before.

The setup for the exercises and homework is excellent, and they provide two tracks A and B to accommodate for specialized players.
Track A: pwn, reverse
Track B: web, crypto, forensics

Check the attached image for the exact contents.

I think that both tracks are pretty introductory, if you really want to compete in the HKUST Firebird team, you should be trying to solve both tracks and learn as much as possible.
comment_teaching:
Although Alex is a great guy, the teaching is conducted by the very handsome COMP4633 students, these students are very experienced in CTF and highly skilled at the fields that they teach.

Although the lectures may last quite a long time (4 hours on Zoom call from 6 to 10 pm) I recommend you don't watch the full 4 hours directly and watch the recording instead.

I also recommend you pause the lecture and try doing the attendance and exercise work by yourself.

The helpers (don't call them TA's) of comp2633 are really quite helpful in helping you complete exercise and homework if you are struggling. They are quite approachable on discord and even during lessons, the vibe is quite chill and some students (not me because I am sleeping at 10pm) may stay back to chat with the lecturer.

I highly recommend taking the time to socialize with these "bigbig" as their experience and advice is invaluable.
comment_workload:
Just passing this course is trivial, copy flag from the lecture videos.

If you want to 100% the exercises and the homework, the workload of this course is equivalent to a 4 credit course.

I took this course along 18 other credits and despite having background in CTF I didn't have enough time to complete all the tasks.

My recommendation for completing the workload is to do the exercises by yourselves while referring to the slides and properly understand the content, and then try to do the homework. Usually the technique needed to solve the homework is covered in the slides.
Otherwise, you can ask the helper in charge for that week about your solve strategy, they won't directly give you the answer, but they will give you some guiding hints that are very useful.
comment_grading:
Very easy course to pass, you just need around 60%?
Since there are two sets of homework, it is actually possible to get 150%.

I am not sure what this means, but It's taken from the course page

comment_content:
This course basically teaches you how to play Capture the Flag (CTF), a kind of cybersecurity competition (as if it isn't clear enough from the course name).

For the first 2 weeks, there are basic trainings that familiarize you with basic CTF skills, including Regex, Linux commands, Python, etc. After that, the course is split in to 2 tracks, namely track A and track B, which are about different topics. Track A is about reverse and binary (pwn), while track B is about web, cryptography (crypto) and a little little little bit of forensics towards the end. Forgive me for being unable to provide detailed teaching content of each category since I don't want to be wrenched by helpers.

Us 2633 students joke about this course being a CS major speedrun, which honestly isn't very far from reality. The content is very broad and deep, so you might want to try really hard and spend a lot of time to understand them if you want to get anything more than a pass.

Little appendix about some terms (even if you don't understand them now, you will eventually understand if you enroll into this course):
Flag: a string that you need to find and submit in order 7to get scores
Reverse: reversing codes from binary files like .exe and find a correct input to get the flag
Pwn: gaining partial / full control of remote systems by exploiting unsafe codes
Web: getting private information by exploiting vulnerabilities in websites / servers
Cryptography: the cryptography you learn in 3632 and/or 4631 idk (not cryptocurrency)
Forensics: retrieving deleted / hidden information inside files
comment_teaching:
Alex is not the one who teaches, it's actually COMP 4633 students (helpers) who do their presentations and teaches us. (they are paying to teach instead of being paid to teach so please send love to your helpers if you are going to enroll in the future UwU :heart::heart::heart:) All of them tried their absolute best, and the powerpoints are very rich in content and clear enough to understand. Unfortunately, they are most likely still not enough for your homework (welcome to the world of cybersecurity), so Google is your best friend.

Lectures are conducted in zoom, and they last for 4 hours (1800-2200). A lecture is separated into 2 halves, 1800-2000 is for track A, 2000-2200 is for track B, so you don't have to attend the whole lecture. After each lecture, lecture videos will be uploaded to the course website, so you can choose to watch videos instead of attending lectures. I highly suggest attending lectures though since you can chill with other students and spam :place_of_worship: in zoom chat.

The overall lecture atmosphere is very casual and relaxing, and you can chat freely with other students, helpers and even management team members who pop out of nowhere during and after lectures. There is a discord server for the HKUST Firebird CTF team and COMP X633 series, which you are highly encouraged to join. You can easily contact other students and helpers to ask for help with in-class exercises and homework, but unless you are completely lost, they usually respond with "try harder" or one or more :blobcattryharder: emojis.
comment_workload:
If you want to find out how the coursework looks like, I suggest you visit the intro website and take a look at the challenges in it: https://intro.firebird.sh/challenges?

==========
"We expect a 3-credit,
COMP 4000-level workload
in this 0-credit course."
~ Alex, at the start of 2633
==========

Alex literally told us that the workload is a solid E. The old course codes for the COMP X633 series are COMP 4901_, which kind of explains the high workload of this course.

All exercises and homework are CTF challenges. For the first 2 weeks, there are 2 exercises and 3 homework each week. For the rest of the course, there are 1 exercise and 1-2 homework for each track each week. Exercise flags can usually be copied from the lecture videos, but you are mostly on your own for homework. For a CTF noob like me, the easier ones usually take me multiple hours, and the harder ones take me multiple days, so unless you are already a CTF god, don't expect yourself to be able to finish all coursework.

In short, only enroll if you are interested. And if you are interested but don't want to be like me who sacrificed all COMP 1021 lesson time for dealing with CTF, you might not want to set your goals too high in this course.
comment_grading:
The highest you can get in this course is 150%, 50% from attendance, 50% from track A and 50% from track B. You only need 60% out of 150% to pass the course so it is extremely easy to pass, but it's quite difficult to get really high scores.

For attendance, you are expected to hand in an attendance flag, which is shown in lecture videos, within 3 days after a lecture, and each flag counts 5%.

Coursework of each week counts 10%, which includes both demo-ed exercises and homework. For each week in the first 2 weeks, 10% is distributed evenly between all challenges. For each week afterwards, track A and track B each counts 5%, and the 5% is evenly distributed between all challenges in the track.

Technically, if you just want to pass, you only need to submit all attendance flags and copy some exercise flags, which don't really take much time. However, if you want to score higher, it's a completely different story. Check the workload part for details.

TL;DR:
Total 150%, Passing 60%/150% (0 cred P/F)
Attendance 50%
Basic training 20%
Track A 40%
Track B 40%

P.S.: If you are interested in 3633 or 4633, you will have to score really really big in 2633 to increase your chances to get into them, as 3633 and 4633 are invitation-based.

comment_grading: This course is only 0 cred and pass/fail only, you only need to get 60% score to pass. Attendance (which makes up 50% of the total score) is taken by inputting a attendance "flag“ into the system within 3 days after the lecture, the attendance flag will be shown in class. There will also be 2 sets of homework and exercises, 1 set for track A and 1 set for track B, all homework+exercise flag for 1 track will make up 50% of the total score, because there is 2 tracks, you can actually get a maximum of 150% in this course, 50% from attendance, 50% for track A exercise + homework, 50% for track B exercise + homework. Most exercises will be demo-ed during lecture, so technically you only need to spend around 15 minutes to find flags in the lecture recording to pass ths course.
